name: ReFi Tanzania: Solar Saving Circles for Sustainable Energy Independence, Financial Sovereignty & Community Resilience
hub: ReFi Tanzania (NEDA Labs), with JUKUMU TZ and The Solar Foundation
place: Mburahati, a dense, hustling neighbourhood of Dar es Salaam where women run the street economy
tagline: Solar lanterns lit the street; the grant round hasn't opened yet
whyItMattered: In a neighbourhood where the grid is unreliable and banks are out of reach, women run the economy on informal savings groups. Giving them a solar asset they own turns saved kerosene money into working capital and extends their trading day past dark.

stats.label: reached any independent local project on-chain

story: When the grant landed, the first call was to Alinagwe Mwaselela, director of JUKUMU TZ and trusted across 200+ savings groups. Together they nominated the strongest-performing women's circles in Mburahati as the first cohort, ran education sessions in Swahili, and handed out 100 solar lanterns.

The lanterns did real work. Women who had never been able to trade after sunset extended their hours; money once spent on kerosene flowed into the shared savings pool instead. Light from the installation even spilled to the nearby police station, where officers on dark overnight shifts said thank you.

The team also built Washikadau, a genuinely live digital hub, and migrating groups onto it from paper notebooks proved slow, patient work. The Bagamoyo Farmers Group authored its own proposal for a solar water pump, with savings and a repayment plan attached.

But the thing the grant was actually funded to do, a local grant round seeding ~$1k each into ten more groups, did not launch. The team says so plainly. On-chain, the ~$4,069 that left the treasury went to the operators' own wallets for lanterns, lab rent and credits; no funds reached an independent local group.

signatureStory.title: The Bagamoyo Farmers Group and the shared water pump
signatureStory.text: Twenty farmers in coastal Bagamoyo, seventeen women and three men, each work a small plot and dread the six-month dry season when there is no reliable water. They dig ponds in the rains, then pay to rent fuel pumps to haul water to their crops. Through the savings circle they began pooling money saved on kerosene with their solar lanterns, watched a SimuSolar demonstration of a solar-powered pump, decided to farm their largest plot together, and submitted their own proposal on Washikadau for the down payment. As of the report the $1,000 was still sitting in the treasury, not yet released.
signatureStory.quote: It is challenging to move water from the ponds to the farm. Each member often has to rent a pump, which is expensive due to fuel and operational costs. Through this group approach, we now have an opportunity to save collectively and invest in a shared solar-powered pump.

delivered: Anchored the work in the JUKUMU network of 200+ savings groups via director Alinagwe Mwaselela
Distributed 100 solar lanterns to women across participating groups, a real and durable household asset
Built and deployed Washikadau, the JUKUMU Digital Hub, a genuinely active platform with a live site and 222-commit repo
Ran Swahili-language education sessions and began migrating groups from paper savings records onto the platform
Paid first rent and began renovating a solar-powered computer-lab and training space at the JUKUMU community hub
Stood up the Bagamoyo Farmers Group solar-pump pipeline with a member-authored proposal and a SimuSolar demonstration

outcomes.text: Women extending trading hours after dark with solar lanterns and redirecting kerosene spend into savings pools (per coordinators' direct observation; independent evidence paywalled)
Solar infrastructure becoming shared neighbourhood good, from the lit police station to an informal gathering point
Groups beginning to move from paper savings records to the Washikadau digital hub
Bagamoyo Farmers Group acquiring a shared solar water pump, funds still held in treasury

status: Active program, grant round not yet launched

verdict.financial: Contradicted — inflows verify, but $0 reached any independent local project; the ~$4,069 outflow went to operator-controlled wallets
verdict.line: A credible local program with real off-chain momentum, but its funded grant round has not launched and $0 reached any independent local project on-chain.

honesty: The lanterns, the live platform, and the farmers'-pump pipeline are real and the team was candid, openly checking 'round not yet launched' and rating Ethereum 'Peripheral.' But the round it was funded to run has not opened, the money that left the treasury went to the operators' own wallets rather than to local groups, ~$2,500 of the bridged grant never returned to the Safe, and the financials do not reconcile.

proposed.summary: Fund Phase 2 of the Solar Saving Circles pilot empowering Mburahati women's savings groups.
points: Run a local grant round seeding ~$1k each into 10 JUKUMU groups
Disburse via NEDApay wallets on community-validated milestone micro-grant proposals
Equip women with solar infrastructure, productive appliances and digital finance training
Stand up a JUKUMU Hub computer lab with grant-operator support

disbursements.purpose: Lanterns, computer-lab and platform costs paid through the team's own wallets — real program spend, but not grants to independent local groups.
Bridged out via a personal wallet and never returned to the Safe; unreconciled in the financial summary.

stories.title: 100 solar lanterns into women's hands
stories.text: The team handed 100 solar lanterns to women across the participating circles. Each lantern displaced costly kerosene and stretched the trading day past sunset. Women who had never sold after dark began earning into the evening, redirecting the saved fuel money straight into their groups' shared savings pools.

stories.title: Savings circles, the social infrastructure of Mburahati
stories.text: In Mburahati, where banks are out of reach, women run the economy on vikoba savings groups. Working through JUKUMU director Alinagwe Mwaselela, trusted across 200+ groups, the team nominated the strongest circles as the first cohort and ran Swahili education sessions on turning solar assets into pooled savings.

dimensionEvidence.evidence: Genuine but ahead of its self-score (independent 3/5 vs self 4/5): the 100 solar lanterns work as income-and-savings assets for 100+ women, kerosene spend is redirected into growing community pools, and a revolving-fund model is emerging via Washikadau. Not higher because the pools and revolving model are still emerging and the seeding round has not run.

Real social capital but over-scored (independent 3/5 vs self 5/5): deep JUKUMU trust, the women's circles, the unplanned police-station spillover, and a group deciding to collectively farm its largest plot. A deliberate strengthening of relationships and trust, held off higher marks because durability is unproven, the round has not launched, and the supporting X evidence is paywalled.

The one dimension the grantee under-scored (independent 3/5 vs self 2/5): a solar water pump set to replace diesel irrigation, a solar-powered community hub and lab, plus 100 lanterns and 5 electric pressure cookers displacing kerosene and LPG — a deliberate community-energy contribution, though short of documented ecological recovery.

correlation.proposed: Run a local grant round seeding ~$1k each into 10 additional JUKUMU groups
correlation.outcome: Round 'not yet launched' by the grantee's own admission; $0 reached any independent local project on-chain. Blocker: migrating users to Washikadau for proposals.

correlation.proposed: Disburse via NEDApay wallets on community-validated milestone micro-grant proposals
correlation.outcome: NEDApay largely absent; the platform became Washikadau and the first 'proposals' came from the 5 operators for their own lanterns and lab. Mechanism shift to internal allocation not framed as a change.

correlation.proposed: Equip women with solar infrastructure, productive appliances and digital finance training
correlation.outcome: 100 solar lanterns and 5 electric pressure cookers distributed; Swahili education sessions run; groups began migrating from paper records onto Washikadau.

correlation.proposed: Stand up a JUKUMU Hub computer lab with grant-operator support
correlation.outcome: Solar-powered computer-lab and training space under renovation, with first rent paid; not yet equipped or fully operational.

correlation.proposed: Savings-circle to productive-asset pipeline (e.g. shared farm equipment)
correlation.outcome: Bagamoyo Farmers Group authored its own Washikadau proposal for a solar water pump after a SimuSolar demo, but the $1,000 is 'in treasury still', not disbursed.

correlation.proposed: Secure $5,000 co-funding to match the grant
correlation.outcome: $5,003 from The Solar Foundation verified on-chain, though it landed 2026-04-01, one day after the report; co-funding base did not broaden beyond the single committed funder.

fundingNote: The funded local grant round never launched, so $0 reached an independent local project. The outflows above are what's known: ~$4,069 went to the team's own operator wallets for lanterns, the computer lab and platform costs, and a further ~$2,500 never returned from a personal-wallet bridge, which the financial summary doesn't reconcile. After the report, a further ~$5,674 left the Safe to operator wallets and a bridge (Apr–May 2026), leaving ~$560.
